_meta.description: GST (federal) and QST (Quebec) sales tax obligations for self-employed workers and small businesses. Covers the $30,000 threshold, registration process, filing, and common exemptions.

title_en: GST/QST — Sales Tax Registration and Collection
title_fr: TPS/TVQ — Inscription et perception des taxes de vente
plain_language_summary: GST is the 5% federal sales tax. QST is Quebec's 9.975% provincial sales tax. Once your business revenue exceeds $30,000 in any 12-month period, you MUST register for both and start collecting them from your customers. Under $30,000, you're a 'small supplier' and registration is optional — but there are reasons to register early.

the_threshold.period: Any 4 consecutive calendar quarters (not just a calendar year)
the_threshold.what_counts: Total worldwide taxable supplies — includes ALL your self-employment income if it's taxable
what_does_not_count: Exempt supplies (certain healthcare, financial services, long-term residential rent)
Zero-rated supplies (basic groceries, prescription drugs, exports)
the_threshold.when_to_register: Within 30 days of exceeding $30,000. You must start collecting on all sales from that date.
the_threshold.penalty_for_late_registration: You're responsible for GST/QST you should have collected even if you didn't — you pay it from your own pocket

rates.example: On a $100 invoice: GST = $5.00, QST = $9.975, total = $114.98

why_register_early: You can claim Input Tax Credits (ITCs) for GST paid on business purchases
You can claim Input Tax Refunds (ITRs) for QST paid on business purchases
Looks more professional to corporate clients
Avoids scrambling when you cross the threshold
why_not_register_early: Adds administrative burden (quarterly or annual filings)
Your prices appear higher if clients can't claim the tax back (B2C sales)
Once registered, you can't easily deregister unless you drop below the threshold
voluntary_registration.recommendation: Freelancers selling to businesses (B2B) should often register early since their clients can reclaim the GST/QST. Retailers and direct-to-consumer sellers can often wait.

gst_registration.where: CRA Business Registration Online or by phone 1-800-959-5525
gst_registration.what_you_get: A Business Number (BN) with a GST/HST account number (format: 123456789 RT 0001)

qst_registration.where: Revenu Québec online via clicSÉQUR or by phone 1-800-567-4692
qst_registration.timeline: 1-2 weeks; QST number arrives by mail

registration_process.register_both_at_same_time: Yes — you can register for GST and QST simultaneously. Revenu Québec coordinates with CRA.

annual.who: Businesses with revenue under $1.5M — can file annually
annual.when: 3 months after fiscal year end (June 15 for December 31 year-end)
annual.note: Annual filers make quarterly installments if they owe more than $3,000

quarterly.who: Businesses with revenue $1.5M–$6M, or those who opt in
quarterly.when: 1 month after each quarter end (April 30, July 31, October 31, January 31)

filing_periods.recommendation_for_micro_business: Annual filing is simplest for micro-entrepreneurs under $1.5M. You only file once a year.

input_tax_credits.plain_language: When you're registered, you can get back the GST/QST you paid on business expenses — this is the main benefit of early registration.
input_tax_credits.how_it_works: Track all GST/QST you paid on business purchases (supplies, equipment, phone, software). Deduct these 'input tax credits' from the GST/QST you collected. Remit only the difference.
input_tax_credits.example: You collected $500 GST from clients. You paid $80 GST on business expenses. You remit $500 - $80 = $420 to CRA.
input_tax_credits.what_qualifies: Any business expense with GST/QST paid, where the expense is for business purposes
input_tax_credits.record_keeping: Keep all receipts for 6 years — CRA can audit back 4 years, Revenu Québec can audit back 4 years

exempt_supplies.plain_language: Some sales are exempt from GST/QST — you don't charge tax on these, and you can't claim input tax credits on related expenses.
examples: Most childcare services — relevant for home daycares
exempt_supplies.home_daycare_note: Home daycare services provided by a recognized RSG are generally exempt from GST/QST. Verify with Revenu Québec for your specific situation.

zero_rated_supplies.plain_language: Zero-rated means 0% GST/QST — but unlike exempt, you CAN claim input tax credits. These are mostly food and exports.
examples: Basic groceries (most food sold in grocery form — NOT restaurant meals or catered food)
zero_rated_supplies.home_baker_note: This is nuanced for bakers. Bread sold as a grocery item may be zero-rated, but a cake sold for a party may be taxable. A dozen muffins sold as groceries may be zero-rated; a single muffin from a bakery counter is taxable. Verify with Revenu Québec for your specific products.

what_to_put_on_invoices: The GST amount charged (or indicate it's included)
The QST amount charged (or indicate it's included)
common_mistakes: Not registering within 30 days of crossing $30,000 — you owe the uncollected tax from your own pocket
Charging GST/QST before being registered — illegal and must be refunded
Forgetting to track input tax credits — you're leaving money on the table
Not keeping receipts — you need proof for every ITC claimed
Confusing zero-rated and exempt — they have different impacts on ITCs
Missing the filing deadline — penalties of 1% of amount owing per month
Not separating GST/QST collected into a separate bank account — it's not your money, set it aside
practical_tip: Open a separate savings account when you register. Every time you issue an invoice with GST/QST, transfer that tax amount to the savings account. When filing time comes, the money is already set aside.
